ideas.The essay as form of thinking:
Michel de Montaigne's most distinctive and enduring innovation was the invention of the **essay** (*essai*) — not merely a new literary genre but a whole new **way of thinking** and writing, one that mirrors the tentative, exploratory, self-questioning movement of an actual human mind at work. The very word he chose is telling: *essai* means an "attempt," a "trial," or a "test," and it perfectly captures the spirit of his enterprise. His writings are not finished treatises delivering settled conclusions, but *attempts* — trials of his own judgment on whatever subject caught his attention.

Montaigne's method was to take up a topic — friendship, cannibals, education, death, thumbs, cruelty, experience — and to *think it through* on the page, following his thoughts wherever they led, digressing freely, changing his mind, contradicting himself, drawing on personal anecdote, classical quotation, and candid self-observation. The essays are famously informal, personal, digressive, and open-ended, reproducing the natural, wandering flow of reflection rather than imposing a rigid logical structure. This was a radical departure from the systematic, authoritative, impersonal treatises of Scholastic philosophy.

Central to this form is its **self-reflective** and provisional character. Montaigne declared that he himself was the subject of his book — "I am myself the matter of my book" — and he used the essay as an instrument of relentless **self-examination**, recording the shifts, inconsistencies, and uncertainties of his own mind with unprecedented honesty. The essay form thus embodies his skeptical, undogmatic outlook: it makes no claim to final truth but stages an ongoing, humble inquiry. This invention proved immensely fertile, founding a literary tradition that runs through Bacon, the great English essayists, and down to the present, and establishing a model of personal, exploratory, honest reflective writing that remains vital.
ideas.What do I know?:
"**What do I know?**" (*Que sais-je?*) is Michel de Montaigne's famous personal motto and the pithy encapsulation of his distinctive brand of **skepticism** — a stance of humble, questioning uncertainty that pervades his *Essays* and that made him one of the most influential skeptical thinkers of the modern era. He had the phrase, along with a set of balancing scales, inscribed as his emblem, and it perfectly captures the spirit of doubt and inquiry that animates his thought.

The question expresses Montaigne's deep suspicion of the human capacity to attain certain **knowledge** — a suspicion he developed most fully in his longest essay, the "Apology for Raymond Sebond," a sustained meditation on the weakness and limits of human reason. There he mounts a battery of skeptical arguments: our senses are unreliable and easily deceived; our reason is fallible and endlessly leads different people (and the same person at different times) to contradictory conclusions; the philosophers have never agreed on anything; human beliefs and customs vary wildly across cultures; and our knowledge is dwarfed by our ignorance. Reviving the ancient **Pyrrhonist** skepticism (which he had encountered in Sextus Empiricus), Montaigne concluded that dogmatic certainty is unwarranted and that the honest response to most great questions is a suspension of judgment.

Crucially, Montaigne's "What do I know?" is not a stance of paralysis or despair but a wise and even *comforting* humility. It counsels intellectual modesty, tolerance of others' views, and openness to experience; it deflates the arrogant pretensions of dogmatists and fanatics (a pressing concern amid the murderous religious wars of his day); and it turns inquiry gently inward, toward honest self-knowledge and the acceptance of uncertainty. This questioning skepticism deeply influenced later thinkers — provoking Descartes's search for certainty, and shaping Pascal, Hume, and the whole modern reflection on the limits of reason.
ideas.Skepticism turned inward:
A defining feature of Montaigne's thought is the way he turned the tools of **skepticism inward**, upon *himself* — transforming the ancient tradition of doubt about the external world and human knowledge into an unprecedented project of honest, searching **self-examination**. If we can be certain of little about the world, Montaigne reasoned, then the one proper and fruitful object of study close at hand is one's own self, in all its shifting, contradictory, and ordinary reality.

Montaigne famously made himself the subject of his book: "I am myself the matter of my book," he wrote, and he pursued the ancient injunction to "know thyself" with a candor and thoroughness new to Western literature. He observed and recorded the constant flux and inconsistency of his own moods, opinions, desires, and judgments — noting how changeable, various, and self-contradictory a single human being is, how differently he feels and thinks from one moment to the next. "We are all patchwork," he observed, "and so shapeless and diverse in composition that each bit, each moment, plays its own game." This attention to the instability and multiplicity of the self was itself a kind of skepticism — a doubt about the coherence and self-transparency of the very "I" that does the knowing.

This inward turn had a liberating and humane result. Rather than despairing over the impossibility of a fixed, certain self-knowledge, Montaigne embraced the ongoing *process* of self-study as an end in itself, and found in it a path to wisdom, self-acceptance, and a gentle, tolerant understanding of the human condition. By studying himself so honestly, he believed, he was also studying **humanity** — "each man bears the entire form of the human condition." This exploration of the intimate, everyday, inconsistent self profoundly shaped the modern understanding of individuality and interiority, and anticipates the introspective traditions of later psychology and literature.
ideas.Acceptance of human variability:
A pervasive theme of Montaigne's *Essays* — and one of the sources of their enduring humanity and appeal — is his profound **acceptance of human variability**: his recognition, and gentle embrace, of the immense **diversity**, **inconsistency**, and **changeableness** of human beings, both across cultures and within a single individual. Against the philosophers who sought fixed, universal rules and the fanatics who imposed rigid certainties, Montaigne celebrated the fluid, various, and unpredictable reality of actual human life.

Montaigne was endlessly struck by how greatly human customs, beliefs, and values **differ from one society to another**. Drawing on his wide reading, on accounts of the newly encountered peoples of the Americas, and on the classical historians, he marveled at the sheer variety of human practices — what one culture holds sacred, another abhors; what seems natural and self-evident to us is merely the accident of local custom. His famous essay "**Of Cannibals**" turns this insight into a pointed cultural critique, suggesting that the "savages" of the New World may be less barbarous than supposedly civilized Europeans, and that we too quickly "call barbarism whatever is not our own practice." This early cultural **relativism** bred in him a deep **tolerance** and a suspicion of ethnocentric arrogance.

Equally, Montaigne accepted the variability *within* each person — the way we are inconstant, contradictory, and different from ourselves at different times. Rather than lamenting this or striving to impose an artificial unity and rigid virtue, he counseled a wise, humble, good-humored **acceptance** of our mixed and imperfect nature, and of the ordinary pleasures and limits of embodied human existence. This tolerant, non-judgmental embrace of human diversity and inconsistency — undogmatic, humane, and worldly — is central to Montaigne's wisdom and to his lasting influence on the modern sensibility.

works.Essays:
The *Essays* (*Essais*, first published 1580 and expanded until Montaigne's death in 1592) is Michel de Montaigne's sole great work — and one of the most original, influential, and beloved books in Western literature. In it Montaigne single-handedly invented the **essay** as a literary form, and created an entirely new kind of book: a vast, sprawling, intimate, and endlessly digressive collection of personal reflections on every conceivable subject, unified only by the presence of the author's own inquiring mind. Its influence on literature, philosophy, and the modern understanding of the self is incalculable.

The book's title announces its method: *essais*, "attempts" or "trials" — for these are not systematic treatises but *attempts* of Montaigne's judgment, thinking-in-progress on the page. Across more than a hundred essays of wildly varying length (from a paragraph to the book-length "Apology for Raymond Sebond"), Montaigne meditates on topics great and small — death, friendship, education, cannibals, cruelty, the imagination, books, experience, sleep, thumbs, and above all himself — following his thoughts freely, quoting the classical authors abundantly, and revising and adding to the text throughout his life so that the *Essays* grew in successive layers.

The animating spirit of the work is Montaigne's famous **skepticism** (his motto "What do I know?"), his humane **tolerance**, and, most distinctively, his unprecedented project of honest **self-examination**: "I am myself the matter of my book," he declares, making his own ordinary, changeable self the primary object of study. Candid, wise, ironic, and companionable, the *Essays* offer no system and no final answers, but a lifelong, humble inquiry into the human condition as reflected in one particular man. Immensely influential — shaping Shakespeare, Pascal, Rousseau, Emerson, and Nietzsche, and founding the essayistic tradition — it remains one of the wisest and most humane books ever written.
